perturbation.original_text:
Either party ("the Terminating Party") may terminate this Agreement upon prior written notice to the other party (the "Defaulting Party") upon the occurrence of any of the following conditions:
(i) In the event that such party materially breaches this Agreement or materially defaults in the performance of any obligation hereunder and fails to cure said breach within 30 days of written notice to the Defaulting Party by the Terminating Party
perturbation.changed_text:
Either party ("the Terminating Party") may terminate this Agreement upon prior written notice to the other party (the "Defaulting Party") upon the occurrence of any of the following conditions:
(i) In the event that such party materially breaches this Agreement or materially defaults in the performance of any obligation hereunder and fails to cure said breach within 10 days of written notice to the Defaulting Party by the Terminating Party
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach from 30 days to 10 days may be unreasonably short and contradict the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in Tennessee contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under Tennessee Law
perturbation.law_citation: Tennessee Code Annotated, Title 47, Chapter 2, Section 309
perturbation.law_explanation: While Tennessee doesn't have a specific statute mandating a minimum cure period, the implied duty of good faith requires parties to act reasonably. A 10-day cure period for a material breach might be deemed unreasonable depending on the complexity of the breach and could be seen as a violation of this implied duty, as it doesn't allow sufficient time to rectify significant issues.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 40: Guidelines for Guardians Ad Litem for Children in Juvenile Court Neglect, Abuse and Dependency Proceedings. | Tennessee Administrative Office of the Courts Skip to main content Rule 40: Guidelines for Guardians Ad Litem for Children in Juvenile Court Neglect, Abuse and Dependency Proceedings. Supreme Court (a) Application. These Guidelines set forth the obligations of lawyers appointed to represent children as guardians ad litem only in juvenile court neglect, abuse and dependency proceedings pursuant to T.C.A. § 37-1-149, Rules 37 of the Tennessee Rules of Juvenile Procedure, and Supreme Court Rule 13. By adoption of these guidelines it is intended that they not be applied to proceedings in other courts that involve child custody or related issues. (b) Definitions. As used in this Rule, unless the context otherwise requires: (1) "Guardian ad litem" is a lawyer appointed by the court to advocate for the best interests of a child and to ensure that the child’s concerns and preferences are effectively advocated. (2) "Child's best interests" refers to a determination of the most appropriate course of action based on objective consideration of the child's specific needs and preferences. In determining the best interest of the child the guardian ad litem should consider, in consultation with experts when appropriate, the following factors: (i) the child’s basic physical needs, such as safety, shelter, food, clothing, and medical care; (ii) the child’s emotional needs, such as nurturance, trust, affection, security, achievement, and encouragement; (iii) the child’s need for family affiliation; (iv) the child’s social needs; (v) the child’s educational needs; (vi) the child’s vulnerability and dependence upon others; (vii) the physical, psychological, emotional, mental, and developmental effects of maltreatment upon the child; (viii) degree of risk; (ix) the child’s need for stability of placement; (x) the child’s age and developmental level, including his or her sense of time; (xi) the general preference of a child to live with known people, to continue normal activities, and to avoid moving; (xii) whether relatives, friends, neighbors, or other people known to the child are appropriate and available as placement resources; (xiii) the love, affection and emotional ties existing between the child and the potential or proposed or competing caregivers; (xiv) the importance of continuity in the child’s life; (xv) the home, school and community record of the child; (xvi) the preferences of the child; (xvii) the willingness and ability of the proposed or potential caretakers to facilitate and encourage close and continuing relationships between the child and other persons in the child’s life with whom the child has or desires to have a positive relationship, including siblings; and (xviii) in the case of visitation or custody disputes between parents, the list of factors set forth in Tenn.Code.Ann. § 36-6-106. (c) General Guidelines. (1) The child is the client of the guardian ad litem
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the cure period for a material breach from 30 to 10 days, which, as the perturbation explanation states, may be unreasonably short and contradict the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in Tennessee contracts. The law explanation supports this by saying that a 10-day cure period might be deemed unreasonable, thus potentially violating the implied duty. Although there are no snippets that directly support a contradiction, the law and perturbation explanations clearly highlight a potential violation of the implied duty of good faith, thus a legal contradiction is created.

perturbation.original_text: Such policies shall provide for at least fifteen (15) days' written notice to Logan's of the cancellation or substantial modification thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Such policies shall provide for at least five (5) days' written notice to Logan's of the cancellation or substantial modification thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the insurance cancellation notice to 5 days violates standard insurance regulations and might be too short a time for Logan's to find replacement coverage.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Insurance Regulations Regarding Cancellation Notice Periods
perturbation.law_citation: Tennessee Department of Commerce and Insurance Regulations, Rule 0780-01-64-.04(2)(b)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the exact notice period depends on the specific insurance type and policy terms, 5 days is unusually short and would be deemed inadequate by many standard policies. Policies often require a minimum of 10 or 15 days.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the written notice period for cancellation or modification of insurance policies from 15 days to 5 days. The Perturbation Explanation states this violates standard insurance regulations. The Law Explanation states that '5 days is unusually short and would be deemed inadequate by many standard policies. Policies often require a minimum of 10 or 15 days'. Although neither snippet provides definitive evidence of a contradiction, the consensus in the explanations points toward a violation in standard insurance regulations.

perturbation.original_text: 6. COMPENSATION. In consideration of the services provided, SRP shall be entitled to a base fee of Eighty Thousand Dollars and No/100's (580,000.00) payable as follows: (i) $40,000.00 payable upon execution of this contract; and (ii) the balance of $40,000.00 payable in eight equal monthly installments of $5,000 each, due the first day of March, April, May, June, July, August, September and October, 1998.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. COMPENSATION. In consideration of the services provided, SRP shall be entitled to a base fee of Eighty Thousand Dollars and No/100's (580,000.00) payable as follows: (i) $40,000.00 payable upon execution of this contract; and (ii) the balance of $40,000.00 payable only at Logan's sole discretion, and if funds are available, with no guaranteed payment schedule.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the payment schedule and making the remaining balance payment discretionary contradicts basic contract law principles and potentially violates Tennessee's Wage Regulations since SRP is providing a service that should have a clear and agreed-upon payment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Tennessee Wage Regulations and Contract Law Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: While this isn't a direct employment relationship, modifying the payment terms to be completely discretionary undermines the certainty required in contractual agreements. Tennessee law requires employers to pay wages promptly and predictably. This change introduces ambiguity that makes it unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Tennessee Code § 50-2-101 (2021) - Prospective Employee to Be Informed as to Wages — Exceptions — Enforcement :: 2021 Tennessee Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Tennessee Code 2023 Tennessee Code 2021 Tennessee Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2021 Tennessee Code Title 50 - Employer and Employee Chapter 2 - Wage Regulations Part 1 - General Provisions § 50-2-101. Prospective Employee to Be Informed as to Wages — Exceptions — Enforcement Universal Citation: TN Code § 50-2-101 (2021) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next As used in this section, “workshops and factories” includes manufacturing, mills, mechanical, electrical, mercantile, art, and laundering establishments, printing, telegraph, and telephone offices, department stores, or any kind of establishment where labor is employed or machinery is used; provided, that domestic service and agricultural pursuits are excluded. It is unlawful for any proprietor, foreman, owner or other person to employ, permit or suffer to work for hire, in, about, or in connection with any workshop or factory any person whatsoever without first informing the employee of the amount of wages to be paid for the labor. This shall not apply to farm labor. Nothing in this section shall apply to railroad companies engaged in interstate commerce and subject to the federal Railway Labor Act, compiled in 45 U.S.C. § 151 et seq. The failure on the part of any proprietor, foreman, owner or other person in charge of any industry named in subsection (a) to inform any employee of the wages to be paid as provided in this section is a Class C misdemeanor. Nothing in this section shall be so construed to preclude the employment of any person or persons on a piece-work basis or on a commission basis. The department of labor and workforce development shall e
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces discretionary payment terms, effectively removing the guaranteed payment schedule outlined in the original contract. This contradicts the principle of certainty in contractual agreements, as the payment is now contingent on Logan's sole discretion and the availability of funds. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 pertains to informing prospective employees of wages, the Law Explanation correctly argues that the discretionary nature of the payment undermines the certainty required in contractual agreements, implying potential non-compliance, especially since SRP is providing a service that expects to be paid for.
